questions.question: What are the four pillars of OOP?
questions.answer:
The four pillars are the core principles that define object-oriented programming. I'll explain each with how I apply them in .NET:

  • Encapsulation — bundle data and behavior together, hide internal implementation behind a public interface. In C#: private fields, public properties with getters/setters. The Portfolio class exposes AddHolding() but hides how it validates and adjusts weights internally.
  • Inheritance — a class (child) inherits fields and behaviors from another class (parent). In C#: the : BaseClass syntax. ApplicationUser : IdentityUser inherits all Identity fields while adding custom ones like DisplayName.
  • Polymorphism — the same interface or method behaves differently for different types. In C#: method overriding (virtual/override), method overloading, interfaces.
  • Abstraction — expose only what's necessary, hide complexity. In C#: interfaces and abstract classes define the "what", concrete implementations define the "how". IEmailSender abstracts SMTP vs SendGrid vs console-logger in tests.
followUps: What is the difference between abstraction and encapsulation?
Can you have OOP without inheritance?
questions.tip: Abstraction vs Encapsulation: Abstraction is about hiding complexity at the design level ("what it does"). Encapsulation is about hiding implementation at the code level ("how it does it"). Interfaces are abstraction; private fields are encapsulation.

questions.question: What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?
questions.answer:
Both define a contract that implementing classes must fulfill, but they have key differences:

FeatureInterfaceAbstract Class
InstantiationCannot instantiateCannot instantiate
Multiple inheritanceClass can implement manyClass can extend only one
State (fields)No instance fields (C# 8: static fields only)Can have instance fields
Default implementationsYes (C# 8+)Yes (non-abstract methods)
ConstructorNoYes (called by derived class)

When I use what:

  • Interface — for defining capabilities that unrelated classes can share. IEmailSender, IProgressService — any class can implement it regardless of its hierarchy. Perfect for DI and mocking.
  • Abstract class — for sharing code among closely related classes. BasePaymentProcessor with shared validation logic, leaving only ProcessPaymentAsync() abstract for subclasses to implement.

In the CSInvoice project, IDocumentGenerate is an interface because unrelated invoice types (Normal, CP, Monthly) all implement the same contract but share no common code.
followUps: When would you choose abstract class over interface?
What are default interface methods in C# 8?
questions.tip: Prefer interfaces over abstract classes for .NET dependency injection — you can only mock/substitute interfaces cleanly. Abstract classes are fine for template method pattern where you're sharing implementation, not just a contract.

questions.question: What is polymorphism and what are its types in C#?
questions.answer:
Polymorphism means "many forms" — the ability to treat different types through a common interface. Two main types in C#:

1. Compile-time (static) polymorphism — method overloading:

public class EmailSender
{
    public void Send(string to, string subject) { ... }
    public void Send(string to, string subject, string template) { ... }
    // Same name, different parameter signatures — resolved at compile time
}

2. Runtime (dynamic) polymorphism — method overriding via virtual/override:

public abstract class NotificationSender
{
    public abstract Task SendAsync(Notification n);
}

public class EmailNotificationSender : NotificationSender
{
    public override async Task SendAsync(Notification n) { /* SMTP logic */ }
}

public class SmsNotificationSender : NotificationSender
{
    public override async Task SendAsync(Notification n) { /* SMS logic */ }
}

// Polymorphic usage — correct Send() called at runtime
NotificationSender sender = GetSender(notificationType);
await sender.SendAsync(notification);
followUps: What is method hiding vs method overriding?
What is operator overloading?
questions.tip: Method hiding vs overriding: override replaces the base method (virtual dispatch). new hides the base method — calling through the base type still calls the base method. Always prefer override; use new only intentionally.

questions.question: What is encapsulation and how do you implement it in C#?
questions.answer:
Encapsulation means bundling data and the methods that operate on it, and restricting direct access to internal state. The class exposes a clean public API while hiding implementation details.

C# access modifiers:

  • public — accessible from anywhere
  • private — accessible only within the class
  • protected — accessible within class and derived classes
  • internal — accessible within the same assembly
  • private protected — derived classes in same assembly only
public class Portfolio
{
    private decimal _totalValue;
    private readonly List<Holding> _holdings = [];

    public decimal TotalValue => _totalValue;  // read-only exposure
    public IReadOnlyList<Holding> Holdings => _holdings.AsReadOnly();

    public void AddHolding(Holding holding)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(holding);
        if (holding.Quantity <= 0)
            throw new ArgumentException("Quantity must be positive");
        _holdings.Add(holding);
        RecalculateTotalValue();
    }

    private void RecalculateTotalValue()  // internal implementation hidden
    {
        _totalValue = _holdings.Sum(h => h.Quantity * h.CurrentPrice);
    }
}
followUps: What is the difference between a field and a property?
What is a readonly field vs a const?
questions.tip: Expose collections as IReadOnlyList<T> — returning the internal List<T> directly lets callers add/remove items bypassing your validation. Return AsReadOnly() or the IReadOnlyList<T> interface.

questions.question: What is inheritance in C# and what are its limitations?
questions.answer:
Inheritance lets a derived class acquire the members (fields, properties, methods) of a base class and extend or override them.

public class Animal
{
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public virtual string Speak() => "...";
}

public class Dog : Animal
{
    public override string Speak() => "Woof!";
}

public sealed class Labrador : Dog  // sealed: cannot be further derived
{
    public override string Speak() => "Woof woof!";
}

Limitations of inheritance:

  • Single inheritance only — C# classes can only extend one class (use interfaces for multiple contracts).
  • Tight coupling — changes to the base class ripple to all derived classes. Fragile base class problem.
  • Can't inherit from sealed class — sealed prevents further derivation (e.g., String is sealed).
  • Inheritance is overused — "Favour composition over inheritance" is a core design principle.
followUps: What is the fragile base class problem?
Composition vs inheritance — when to use which?
questions.tip: Composition over Inheritance rule of thumb: Ask "is a" vs "has a". Dog IS AN Animal → inheritance. Car HAS AN Engine → composition. If in doubt, compose — it's easier to change later.

questions.question: What are generics in C# and why are they useful?
questions.answer:
Generics allow you to write type-safe, reusable code without specifying the actual type until the code is used. The compiler enforces type safety at compile time (no boxing/unboxing, no casting).

// Generic repository — works for any entity type
public interface IRepository<T> where T : class
{
    Task<T?> GetByIdAsync(int id);
    Task<IReadOnlyList<T>> GetAllAsync();
    Task AddAsync(T entity);
}

// Generic result wrapper — avoids exception-driven flow
public class Result<T>
{
    public bool IsSuccess { get; private init; }
    public T? Value { get; private init; }
    public string? Error { get; private init; }

    public static Result<T> Success(T value) =>
        new() { IsSuccess = true, Value = value };

    public static Result<T> Failure(string error) =>
        new() { IsSuccess = false, Error = error };
}

// Generic constraints
public class Repository<T> where T : class, IEntity, new() { ... }

Generic constraints: where T : class (reference type), where T : struct (value type), where T : new() (parameterless constructor), where T : IEntity (must implement interface).
followUps: What is a generic constraint?
What is the difference between covariance and contravariance?
questions.tip: Covariance (out T) — can use a more derived type. IEnumerable<Dog> can be assigned to IEnumerable<Animal>. Contravariance (in T) — can use a less derived type. Used on method parameters. Most developers just need to know generic collections are covariant when the type parameter is out.

questions.question: What are delegates and events in C#?
questions.answer:
A delegate is a type-safe function pointer — a reference to a method that matches a specific signature. Action, Func<T>, and Predicate<T> are built-in delegates.

// Built-in delegates
Func<int, bool> isEven = x => x % 2 == 0;
Action<string> log = Console.WriteLine;

// LINQ uses delegates extensively
var activeItems = items.Where(p => p.IsActive);  // Func<T, bool>

// Event (restricts direct invocation — only class can raise it)
public class PaymentProcessor
{
    public event EventHandler<PaymentCompletedEventArgs>? PaymentCompleted;

    protected virtual void OnPaymentCompleted(PaymentCompletedEventArgs e)
        => PaymentCompleted?.Invoke(this, e);
}

// Subscriber
paymentProcessor.PaymentCompleted += async (sender, e) =>
    await SendReceiptEmailAsync(e.Invoice);

An event wraps a delegate with add/remove accessors — only the declaring class can raise it. Implements the Observer pattern.
followUps: What is the difference between event and delegate?
What is EventHandler<T>?
What are lambda expressions?
questions.tip: Events vs delegates: A public delegate field lets anyone invoke it. An event wraps the delegate and restricts external code to += and -= only — the raising is encapsulated inside the class. Always use events for the observer pattern.

questions.question: What is the difference between value types and reference types in C#?
questions.answer:
Value types — stored directly where declared (stack for locals). Copying creates an independent copy. Includes: int, double, bool, DateTime, struct, enum.

Reference types — variable holds a reference (pointer) to heap-allocated data. Copying copies the reference, not the data. Includes: class, string, array, interface.

// Value type: copy semantics
DateTime d1 = DateTime.Now;
DateTime d2 = d1;  // independent copy
d2 = d2.AddDays(1);  // d1 unchanged

// Reference type: shared reference
var list1 = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
var list2 = list1;  // both point to same list!
list2.Add(4);  // list1 also has 4!

// String is reference type but behaves like value type (immutable)
string s1 = "hello";
string s2 = s1;
s2 += " world";  // creates a new string, s1 unchanged

// Boxing: value type → object (heap allocation)
object boxed = 42;        // boxing
int unboxed = (int)boxed; // unboxing — expensive, avoid in hot paths
followUps: What is a nullable value type?
What are C# records?
What is struct vs class performance trade-off?
questions.tip: Structs are copied by value — passing a large struct to a method copies every field. Use in parameter modifier to pass readonly reference: void Process(in LargeStruct s). Or just use class if it's large or mutable.

questions.question: What is CLR and how does .NET code execute?
questions.answer:
CLR (Common Language Runtime) is the virtual machine that executes .NET code. It provides: memory management (GC), type safety, exception handling, thread management, and security. When you write C#, the compiler produces IL (Intermediate Language) — not native machine code. The CLR then converts IL to native code at runtime via the JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler.

Execution flow:

  1. You write C# → C# compiler produces IL (in .dll/.exe)
  2. Runtime: CLR's JIT compiler converts IL to native machine code on first execution of a method
  3. Native code is cached in memory — subsequent calls run native code directly (no re-JIT)
  4. GC automatically manages object lifetimes

Key CLR services:

  • Garbage Collector — automatic memory management, generational (Gen0/Gen1/Gen2/LOH)
  • Type safety verifier — prevents illegal memory access
  • Exception handling — structured try/catch/finally across all .NET languages
  • Thread support — ThreadPool, Tasks, async/await plumbing

In .NET 8, the ReadyToRun (R2R) compilation model pre-compiles IL to native code at publish time, reducing JIT startup overhead — important for containerized microservices with cold starts.
followUps: What is Native AOT in .NET 8?
What is Roslyn?
What is the difference between .NET CLR and Java JVM?
questions.tip: Native AOT (.NET 7+) compiles the entire application to native machine code at build time — no JIT at runtime. Startup time drops from ~100ms to single-digit ms, memory footprint shrinks. Trade-off: no runtime code generation (no Reflection.Emit), larger binary.

questions.question: How does Garbage Collection work in .NET? What are generations?
questions.answer:
Garbage Collection (GC) is the CLR's automatic memory manager. It reclaims memory occupied by unreachable objects (objects with no live references) so you don't have to manually free memory.

Generational GC — the key concept:

  • Gen0 — short-lived objects (local variables, temporary allocations). Collected most frequently. Fast — typically takes <1ms.
  • Gen1 — objects that survived Gen0 collection. Buffer between Gen0 and Gen2.
  • Gen2 — long-lived objects (static fields, large caches, DbContext). Collected rarely. Expensive — can take 100ms+.
  • LOH (Large Object Heap) — objects >85KB allocated directly here. Only collected during Gen2 (full GC).
// Prefer object pooling for frequently allocated large objects
var pool = new ObjectPool<LargeBuffer>(() => new LargeBuffer());
var buffer = pool.Get();
try {
    // use buffer
} finally {
    pool.Return(buffer); // reuse instead of GC
}

// Dispose patterns trigger GC-eligible state
public class DatabaseContext : IDisposable
{
    private bool _disposed;

    public void Dispose()
    {
        if (!_disposed) {
            _connection?.Dispose();
            _disposed = true;
        }
        GC.SuppressFinalize(this);  // don't need finalizer now
    }
}

In FolioTrack, I kept FundHoldingsService as a Singleton and cached fund data in-memory (Gen2) — intentional, infrequently updated. Short-lived DTO projections stay in Gen0 and are collected quickly.
followUps: What is IDisposable and when should you implement it?
What is a finalizer and when is it called?
How do you diagnose a memory leak in .NET?
questions.tip: GC pressure rule of thumb: Don't fight the GC — it's well-optimized for short-lived objects. Optimize when profiling shows Gen2 collections happening frequently (full GC). Use dotnet-counters or Application Insights to monitor GC stats in production.

questions.question: What is the difference between var and dynamic in C#?
questions.answer:
Both allow you to skip explicit type declarations, but they work completely differently:

Featurevardynamic
Type resolutionCompile time (inferred from RHS)Runtime (late binding)
Type safetyFully type-safeNo compile-time checking
IntelliSenseFull IntelliSenseNo IntelliSense
PerformanceSame as explicit typeSlower (DLR overhead)
// var: compiler infers type at compile time
var name = "Alice";     // string — fully type-safe
var count = items.Where(x => x.Active).Count();  // int
name = 42;              // COMPILER ERROR: can't assign int to string

// dynamic: resolved at runtime — dangerous
dynamic obj = "hello";
obj = 42;               // no error — type changes at runtime!
obj.NonExistentMethod(); // runtime exception: RuntimeBinderException

// Valid use of dynamic: COM interop, reflection simplification
dynamic excel = Activator.CreateInstance(Type.GetTypeFromProgID("Excel.Application"));
excel.Visible = true;  // no need to cast

I use var everywhere for readability. dynamic is rare — only for COM interop or deserializing unknown JSON shapes. In all my projects (FolioTrack, ServiceSelection) I avoid dynamic.
followUps: Can you use var for method parameters?
What is the DLR (Dynamic Language Runtime)?
questions.tip: var is NOT the same as dynamic. var is just syntactic sugar — the actual type is known at compile time. dynamic bypasses compile-time type checking entirely. Always prefer var over dynamic unless you specifically need late binding.

questions.question: What is the difference between const, readonly, and static readonly?
questions.answer:
All three create fields that can't be arbitrarily changed, but their timing and scope differ:

Featureconstreadonlystatic readonly
When setCompile timeConstructor or initializerStatic constructor or initializer
Can change per instanceNoYes (in constructor)No (single value)
Runtime value allowedNo (literal only)YesYes
public class Config
{
    // const: baked into calling assembly at compile time
    public const int MaxRetries = 3;  // must be a literal
    public const string DefaultCulture = "en-US";

    // readonly: set once in constructor, can vary per instance
    private readonly string _connectionString;
    public Config(string cs) { _connectionString = cs; }

    // static readonly: set once at class initialization, runtime value OK
    public static readonly DateTime AppStartTime = DateTime.UtcNow;
    public static readonly string Version =
        Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly().GetName().Version!.ToString();
}

Key gotcha with const: If you change a const value in assembly A, assembly B must be recompiled to see the new value — the old value was baked in at compile time. Use static readonly if the value might change between versions.
followUps: What is a readonly struct?
When would you use const vs static readonly?
questions.tip: Use static readonly instead of const for public API values that might change between library versions. const is baked into the calling assembly — a version bump without recompile will use the old value. static readonly is evaluated fresh at runtime.

questions.question: What is the IDisposable pattern and when do you implement it?
questions.answer:
IDisposable is a contract for releasing unmanaged resources (file handles, database connections, network sockets) deterministically — without waiting for the GC. Implement it when your class holds resources that must be freed promptly.

public class ReportGenerator : IDisposable
{
    private Stream? _outputStream;
    private bool _disposed;

    public ReportGenerator(string filePath)
    {
        _outputStream = File.Create(filePath);
    }

    public void GenerateReport() { /* write to stream */ }

    public void Dispose()
    {
        Dispose(disposing: true);
        GC.SuppressFinalize(this); // finalizer not needed
    }

    protected virtual void Dispose(bool disposing)
    {
        if (!_disposed)
        {
            if (disposing)
                _outputStream?.Dispose();
            _disposed = true;
        }
    }
}

// Usage — C# using statement guarantees Dispose() is called
using var generator = new ReportGenerator("report.pdf");
generator.GenerateReport();
// Dispose() called automatically at end of using block

// C# 8+ using declaration (same behavior, less nesting)
using var conn = new SqlConnection(connectionString);

The using statement is syntactic sugar for a try/finally block that calls Dispose(). In all my projects, any class inheriting from EF Core's DbContext, SqlConnection, or HttpClient flows through using blocks.
followUps: What is the difference between Dispose and Finalize?
What does GC.SuppressFinalize do?
What is IAsyncDisposable?
questions.tip: In ASP.NET Core DI, registered services are automatically disposed at the end of the request (Scoped) or app shutdown (Singleton). You still need using for objects created manually (new SqlConnection, new StreamReader, etc.).

questions.question: What is the difference between string and StringBuilder in C#?
questions.answer:
string is immutable — every concatenation or modification creates a new string object on the heap. In a loop, this generates enormous GC pressure.

StringBuilder is mutable — it maintains an internal buffer and appends without creating new objects each time.

// BAD: creates 1000 string objects
string result = "";
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
    result += i.ToString();  // each += allocates a new string!

// GOOD: one StringBuilder, minimal allocations
var sb = new StringBuilder(capacity: 5000); // pre-size for efficiency
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
    sb.Append(i);
string result = sb.ToString();  // single string created at end

// Real-world example: email body builder in PaymentReminder service
var emailBody = new StringBuilder();
emailBody.AppendLine("Dear ").Append(client.Name).AppendLine(",");
emailBody.AppendLine($"Your invoice #{inv.Id} of {inv.Amount:C} is due.");
emailBody.AppendLine("Regards, Billing Team");
string body = emailBody.ToString();

When to use what:

  • string — concatenating 2-3 strings, string literals, simple operations
  • StringBuilder — concatenating in loops, building large strings, HTML/email body generation
  • string.Join() — joining a known collection — fastest option, internally uses efficient buffer
followUps: What is string interning?
What is Span<char> for string operations?
questions.tip: string.Join is often faster than StringBuilder for collections: string.Join(", ", items) is cleaner and optimized. Use StringBuilder when you're building a string step-by-step with conditional logic.

questions.question: What is a sealed class and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A sealed class cannot be inherited — it terminates the inheritance chain. A sealed method in an unsealed class prevents further overriding in derived classes.

// Sealed class — no further inheritance allowed
public sealed class JwtHelper
{
    private readonly string _secret;
    public JwtHelper(string secret) { _secret = secret; }

    public string GenerateToken(int userId, string email)
    {
        // JWT generation logic
    }
}

// Cannot extend: public class ExtendedJwtHelper : JwtHelper {} // COMPILE ERROR

// Sealed method — prevent overriding in further derived classes
public class PaymentBase
{
    protected virtual void Validate() { ... }
}

public class CreditCardPayment : PaymentBase
{
    public sealed override void Validate() { ... } // cannot be overridden further
}

When to use sealed:

  • Classes designed as utilities with no extension points (JwtHelper, static-like classes)
  • Performance-critical classes — JIT can devirtualize calls on sealed classes (inlining opportunity)
  • Security — prevent substitution attacks via inheritance
  • Value types (structs) are implicitly sealed
followUps: Does sealing a class improve performance?
Why is string sealed in .NET?
questions.tip: String is sealed for safety: If string were extensible, an attacker could create a malicious subclass with a modified GetHashCode() or Equals() — breaking Dictionary lookups and security checks. Many framework types are sealed for the same reason.

questions.question: What is exception handling in C#? What is the difference between throw and throw ex?
questions.answer:
Exception handling in C# uses try-catch-finally blocks. The try block contains code that might throw. The catch block handles specific exception types. The finally block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred — used for cleanup.

// try-catch-finally pattern
public async Task<Invoice> GenerateInvoiceAsync(int assignmentId)
{
    SqlConnection? conn = null;
    try
    {
        conn = new SqlConnection(_connectionString);
        await conn.OpenAsync();
        // ... invoice generation
        return invoice;
    }
    catch (SqlException ex) when (ex.Number == 1205)  // deadlock
    {
        _logger.LogWarning("Deadlock on invoice {Id}, retrying", assignmentId);
        throw;  // rethrow — preserve stack trace
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Invoice generation failed for {Id}", assignmentId);
        throw;  // CORRECT: throw preserves original stack trace
        // throw ex; // WRONG: resets stack trace to this line
    }
    finally
    {
        conn?.Dispose(); // always runs
    }
}

throw vs throw ex:

  • throw — rethrows the exception preserving the original stack trace (where it actually happened)
  • throw ex — resets the stack trace to the current line — you lose the original call stack, making debugging much harder

Exception filters (catch (Exception ex) when (condition)) — the filter runs before the stack unwinds, preserving the full stack trace while allowing conditional handling.
followUps: What is AggregateException?
What is custom exception creation?
What is exception filter?
questions.tip: Always use throw not throw ex when rethrowing. The stack trace is your most valuable debugging tool in production. Losing it with throw ex forces you to reproduce the bug rather than reading the trace.

questions.question: What are lambda expressions and how are they used in C#?
questions.answer:
A lambda expression is an anonymous function — a short inline function that can be assigned to a delegate or passed as a method argument. Syntax: parameters => expression.

// Basic lambda
Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
Console.WriteLine(square(5)); // 25

// Multi-parameter
Func<int, int, int> add = (a, b) => a + b;

// Statement lambda (block body)
Func<int, bool> isPrime = n => {
    if (n < 2) return false;
    for (int i = 2; i <= Math.Sqrt(n); i++)
        if (n % i == 0) return false;
    return true;
};

// In LINQ (most common usage)
var activePortfolios = portfolios
    .Where(p => p.IsActive)                     // filter
    .Select(p => new { p.Id, p.Name })           // project
    .OrderByDescending(p => p.CreatedAt)          // sort
    .ToList();

// In event handlers
btnSave.Click += async (sender, e) => await SaveAsync();

// Capturing variables (closure)
string prefix = "Hello";
Action<string> greet = name => Console.WriteLine($"{prefix}, {name}!"); // captures prefix
greet("Alice"); // "Hello, Alice!"

// C# 9+ shorter lambda for property access
var names = people.Select(p => p.Name).ToList(); // common pattern

Lambdas are the backbone of LINQ and make collection operations concise. I use them throughout FolioTrack's service layer for filtering and projecting EF Core queries.
followUps: What is the difference between Func and Action?
What is a closure and what are the capture gotchas?
questions.tip: Closure capture gotcha with loops: Capturing a loop variable in a lambda captures the variable, not its current value. By the time the lambda executes, the loop may have finished and the variable holds the last value. Fix: var captured = loopVar; var lambda = () => Use(captured);
